Energy Unit 	© It’s Not Rocket Science 2017 	7
Lab Activity: Exploring the Motion of a Pendulum
Guiding Question: How does the change in motion of a
pendulum ride at a fair or amusement park affect energy?
Purpose: To determine the relationship between energy and
motion by investigating an amusement park ride.
Introduction: Have you ever been to a fair or an amusement
park and seen a Viking ship ride like the one pictured to the
right? Better yet, have you ever been on one of these rides? If
you haven’t, these rides basically act like giant pendulums,
swinging you back and forth. If you have, you can attest that as
the ride cranks you up to the side, there is a moment of stillness
(and terror) right before it drops you in a giant swinging motion,
back and forth until the ride comes to an end. Rides like this can
tell us a lot about the relationship between motion and energy,
which is why we will be exploring them today.
Hypothesis: Write a prediction (it can be in an informal format
for this activity) about the relationship between energy and
motion that you think exists on rides such as this.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Materials: (per group)
• 	3 washers
• 	1 piece of yarn
• 	1-2 meter sticks
• 	1 stopwatch or timer
• 	Digital scale
Procedure:
1. 	Find the mass of 1 washer using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 1.
2. 	Find the mass of all 3 washers together using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 2.
3. 	Measure and cut your piece of yarn to be about 0.6 m (60 cm) long.
4. 	Tie one end to 1 washer. Lay flat. Now measure the length from the bottom of the washer to the other end of the
yarn. Trim off the end until the entire length of the yarn AND washer is 0.5 m (50 cm). You now have your first
pendulum! This will be used to simulate the motion of rides at the amusement park, like the one pictured above.
5. 	Group member #1 should be standing, holding the meter stick vertically, with the zero end resting on the ground.
He/she should also be holding the string end of the pendulum at 0.5 m high. If being held correctly, the washer
should be almost touching the ground, but not quite (we don’t want it to hit the ground and have that impact its
motion, but we do want it to swing as close to the ground as possible.)
6. 	Group member #2 should be sitting on the ground with the stopwatch, ready to take the time.
7. 	Group member #3 should pick up the washer, being careful not to pull it out of person #1’s hand. It should be lifted
outward so that it is parallel to the ground and perpendicular to the meter stick. Measure the height from the washer to
the ground with another meter stick (if you have one.) If it is being held correctly, you should get a height of 0.5 m.
8. 	On “go”, person #3 should let go of the washer and person #2 should start the stopwatch, observing the motion of the
pendulum. Person #2 should stop the stopwatch when the pendulum passes just above the ground before continuing
in its path upwards. It may take a few tries to get it. Record the time in Data Table 1. Repeat for 3 trials.
9. 	Repeat steps 3-8, this time tying all 3 washers to your pendulum. Record results for all 3 trials in Data Table 2.
10. Calculate average times in Data Tables 1 and 2. Then answer all analysis questions.
Answers will vary

**Thinking Ahead: What do you think the relationship is between kinetic and potential energy in systems like

Kinetic energy, because the pendulum is now in motion and all objects in motion have kinetic
energy
When it had the greatest height, which is at the beginning
It should have the most kinetic energy when it is moving the fastest (or having the lowest GPE)
which should be at the bottom of its swing before it starts swinging upwards again
Answers will vary, but should be able to find the peak velocity at the bottom of the swing using
acceleration and average time to rearrange for final velocity (initial would be zero). (You may

#1 added more mass or #2 dropped them from a greater height so they would start off with
more GPE (that will convert to KE as it falls, but they won't understand this yet!)
All of the kinetic energy came from the initial gravitational potential energy
As one goes up, the other goes down! (Ex. as the pendulum fell, GPE decreased and KE
increased)

Lab Investigation: Law of Conservation of Energy
Guiding Question: How does energy transform, with respect to the law of conservation of energy, when a ball is in
motion?
Purpose: To investigate energy transformations in a falling ball with respect to the law of conservation of energy.
Hypothesis:
Make a prediction about which ball you believe will have the most initial energy and will retain the most energy when it
bounces.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Materials:
• 	4 different balls
• 	Meter stick
• 	Digital scale
Procedures:
1. 	Find the mass of one of the balls. Record in Data Table 1.
2. 	Have one member of the group hold the meter stick upright, with the zero end on the floor. Hold the ball at the
top of the meter stick (1 m from the ground.)
3. 	Another member of the group should squat down to eye level with the meter stick. When the ball is dropped,
measure the height of the ball after the 1 st bounce. Record in Data Table 2.
4. 	Drop the ball again, this time recording the height of the 2 nd bounce. Make sure the same person measures the
height each time for consistency. Re-do the drop if you don’t think an accurate measurement was made.
5. 	Drop the ball again and record the height of the 3 rd bounce.
6. 	Repeat steps 1-5 for all four balls.

how the height of each bounce always decreased
It should have bounced back to the original height of 1 m, according to the law of conservation of
energy. Actually saw that each bounce was a lower height, suggesting that not all energy was

Because according to the law of conservation of energy, energy can not be gained (or destroyed)
It doesn't mean that the law can be violated or isn't true. It just means that the energy must be

Practice: Thermal Energy Calculations
Answer the following questions. Make sure to show all work for the math problems to receive credit. You may need a
separate sheet of paper.
1. 	Explain the relationship between temperature, energy, and motion of particles in an object.
2. 	Referencing the reasoning you used from #1, explain the difference between objects that feel hot and those that
feel “cold”.
3. 	You’ve been waiting for the bus and your hands become cold. When you get onto the bus and sit down, you put
your hands under your legs to warm up. After a while your hands feel warmer but your legs feel colder. Explain
this with regards to what you know about thermal energy transfer.
4. 	How much energy must be absorbed by water with a mass of 0.5 kg in order to raise the temperature from 30°C to
65°C? Note: Water has a specific heat of 4,190 J/kg °C.
5. 	How much heat is needed to warm .052 kg of gold from 30°C to 120°C? Note: Gold has a specific heat of 136
J/kg °C.
6. 	A 9.5 kg outdoor copper sculpture heats up during the day from 24°C to 78°C. How much energy was absorbed?
Note: Copper has a specific heat of 390 J/kg °C.
7. 	Challenge: If it takes 820 Joules of heat to warm a sample of zinc from 0°C to 50°C, what would be the mass of
the zinc? Note: Zinc has a specific heat of 380 J/kg °C.
Temperature is the average kinetic energy of particles in an object
So, the higher the temperature, the greater the kinetic energy of the particles, therefore the
faster the particles are moving
Objects that feel hot have more heat, because they have a greater temperature and thus a
greater kinetic energy in their particles. Objects that feel "cold" have less heat, because they
have a lower temp. and thus lower KE in their particles.
Your body is warmer than your hands, thus has a higher temperature and more heat. Heat flows
from higher temperature objects to lower temperature objects, so heat will leave your legs and
move to your hands, thus you feel the absence of heat in your legs.

Introduction: Rebecca loves to have ice cold water on her bedside table to drink when she wakes up in the
middle of the night. She has been learning in physical science class about thermal energy transfer, and wants to
figure out a way to create a homemade insulator for her ice water. Can you help her?

Answers will vary.

Her ice keeps melting because her cup is allowing heat into the cup

Good insulators = plastic, foam, rubber, etc.
Bad insulators (aka good conductors) = metal, air, skin

Lab Activity: Exploring the Motion of a Pendulum
Guiding Question: How does the change in motion of a
pendulum ride at a fair or amusement park affect energy?
Purpose: To determine the relationship between energy and
motion by investigating an amusement park ride.
Introduction: Have you ever been to a fair or an amusement
park and seen a Viking ship ride like the one pictured to the
right? Better yet, have you ever been on one of these rides? If
you haven’t, these rides basically act like giant pendulums,
swinging you back and forth. If you have, you can attest that as
the ride cranks you up to the side, there is a moment of stillness
(and terror) right before it drops you in a giant swinging motion,
back and forth until the ride comes to an end. Rides like this can
tell us a lot about the relationship between motion and energy,
which is why we will be exploring them today.
Hypothesis: Write a prediction (it can be in an informal format
for this activity) about the relationship between energy and
motion that you think exists on rides such as this.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Materials: (per group)
• 	3 washers
• 	1 piece of yarn
• 	1-2 meter sticks
• 	1 stopwatch or timer
• 	Digital scale
Procedure:
1. 	Find the mass of 1 washer using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 1.
2. 	Find the mass of all 3 washers together using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 2.
3. 	Measure and cut your piece of yarn to be about 0.6 m (60 cm) long.
4. 	Tie one end to 1 washer. Lay flat. Now measure the length from the bottom of the washer to the other end of the
yarn. Trim off the end until the entire length of the yarn AND washer is 0.5 m (50 cm). You now have your first
pendulum! This will be used to simulate the motion of rides at the amusement park, like the one pictured above.
5. 	Group member #1 should be standing, holding the meter stick vertically, with the zero end resting on the ground.
He/she should also be holding the string end of the pendulum at 0.5 m high. If being held correctly, the washer
should be almost touching the ground, but not quite (we don’t want it to hit the ground and have that impact its
motion, but we do want it to swing as close to the ground as possible.)
6. 	Group member #2 should be sitting on the ground with the stopwatch, ready to take the time.
7. 	Group member #3 should pick up the washer, being careful not to pull it out of person #1’s hand. It should be lifted
outward so that it is parallel to the ground and perpendicular to the meter stick. Measure the height from the washer to
the ground with another meter stick (if you have one.) If it is being held correctly, you should get a height of 0.5 m.
8. 	On “go”, person #3 should let go of the washer and person #2 should start the stopwatch, observing the motion of the
pendulum. Person #2 should stop the stopwatch when the pendulum passes just above the ground before continuing
in its path upwards. It may take a few tries to get it. Record the time in Data Table 1. Repeat for 3 trials.
9. 	Repeat steps 3-8, this time tying all 3 washers to your pendulum. Record results for all 3 trials in Data Table 2.
10. Calculate average times in Data Tables 1 and 2. Then answer all analysis questions.
Answers will vary

Temperature is the average kinetic energy of particles in an object
So, the higher the temperature, the greater the kinetic energy of the particles, therefore the
faster the particles are moving
Objects that feel hot have more heat, because they have a greater temperature and thus a
greater kinetic energy in their particles. Objects that feel "cold" have less heat, because they
have a lower temp. and thus lower KE in their particles.
Your body is warmer than your hands, thus has a higher temperature and more heat. Heat flows
from higher temperature objects to lower temperature objects, so heat will leave your legs and
move to your hands, thus you feel the absence of heat in your legs.

Lab Activity: Exploring the Motion of a Pendulum
Guiding Question: How does the change in motion of a
pendulum ride at a fair or amusement park affect energy?
Purpose: To determine the relationship between energy and
motion by investigating an amusement park ride.
Introduction: Have you ever been to a fair or an amusement
park and seen a Viking ship ride like the one pictured to the
right? Better yet, have you ever been on one of these rides? If
you haven’t, these rides basically act like giant pendulums,
swinging you back and forth. If you have, you can attest that as
the ride cranks you up to the side, there is a moment of stillness
(and terror) right before it drops you in a giant swinging motion,
back and forth until the ride comes to an end. Rides like this can
tell us a lot about the relationship between motion and energy,
which is why we will be exploring them today.
Hypothesis: Write a prediction (it can be in an informal format
for this activity) about the relationship between energy and
motion that you think exists on rides such as this.
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
__________________________________________________________________________________________________
Materials: (per group)
• 	3 washers
• 	1 piece of yarn
• 	1-2 meter sticks
• 	1 stopwatch or timer
• 	Digital scale
Procedure:
1. 	Find the mass of 1 washer using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 1.
2. 	Find the mass of all 3 washers together using the digital scale. Record in Data Table 2.
3. 	Measure and cut your piece of yarn to be about 0.6 m (60 cm) long.
4. 	Tie one end to 1 washer. Lay flat. Now measure the length from the bottom of the washer to the other end of the
yarn. Trim off the end until the entire length of the yarn AND washer is 0.5 m (50 cm). You now have your first
pendulum! This will be used to simulate the motion of rides at the amusement park, like the one pictured above.
5. 	Group member #1 should be standing, holding the meter stick vertically, with the zero end resting on the ground.
He/she should also be holding the string end of the pendulum at 0.5 m high. If being held correctly, the washer
should be almost touching the ground, but not quite (we don’t want it to hit the ground and have that impact its
motion, but we do want it to swing as close to the ground as possible.)
6. 	Group member #2 should be sitting on the ground with the stopwatch, ready to take the time.
7. 	Group member #3 should pick up the washer, being careful not to pull it out of person #1’s hand. It should be lifted
outward so that it is parallel to the ground and perpendicular to the meter stick. Measure the height from the washer to
the ground with another meter stick (if you have one.) If it is being held correctly, you should get a height of 0.5 m.
8. 	On “go”, person #3 should let go of the washer and person #2 should start the stopwatch, observing the motion of the
pendulum. Person #2 should stop the stopwatch when the pendulum passes just above the ground before continuing
in its path upwards. It may take a few tries to get it. Record the time in Data Table 1. Repeat for 3 trials.
9. 	Repeat steps 3-8, this time tying all 3 washers to your pendulum. Record results for all 3 trials in Data Table 2.
10. Calculate average times in Data Tables 1 and 2. Then answer all analysis questions.
Answers will vary

middle of the night. She has been learning in physical science class about thermal energy transfer, and wants to
figure out a way to create a homemade insulator for her ice water. Can you help her?

Students should know...
Thermal energy (heat) transfers from high temp to low temp
Her ice keeps melting because her cup is allowing heat into the cup
It can be prevented by creating an insulating cup that keeps heat out
Good insulators = plastic, foam, rubber, etc.
Bad insulators (aka good conductors) = metal, air, skin

Practice: Work and Power Calculations
Answer the following questions. Make sure to show all work to receive credit. You may need a separate sheet of paper.
1. 	It took 500 N of force to push a car 4 meters. How much work was done?
2. 	A force of 15 N is used to push a box along the floor a distance of 3 meters. Find the amount of work
done.
3. 	A force of 100 N was needed to lift a rock. A total of 150 J of work was done. How far was the rock
lifted?
4. 	Max exerted a force of 9,000 N on a stalled car but was unable to move it. How much work did he do?
5. 	A 5 kg rock is lifted 2 m. Find the amount of work done.
6. 	How much power is used if a force of 35 N is used to push a box a distance of 10 meters in 5 seconds?
7. 	If 270 watts of power is used in 42 seconds, how much work was done?
8. 	How long does it take to do 278 J of work with 17 W of power?
9. 	A pulley is used to lift a piano weighing 1,000 N. The piano is lifted 3 m in 60 s. How much power is
used?
